items.question_text: When may you provide radar service?

choices.text: Only when you are personally satisfied that the radar presentation and equipment performance are adequate for the service being provided.
Whenever radar targets are visible, even if equipment performance is questionable.
Only when the system has full primary and secondary coverage to the edge of the facility boundary.
Whenever the pilot prefers radar service over nonradar procedures.

items.explanation: Radar service depends on the controller being satisfied that the presentation and equipment performance are adequate for the service in use.

items.question_text: Outside Class A airspace, when may secondary radar be used as the sole display source?

choices.text: Only when additional coverage is provided beyond primary radar, or the primary radar is temporarily unusable or out of service, and the associated conditions are met.
Any time a transponder target is stronger than the primary return.
Only when pilots specifically request transponder-only service.
Whenever the facility is operating in FUSION mode, with no other limitation.

items.explanation: Secondary-only use outside Class A is limited to the cases listed in the paragraph, not general convenience.

items.question_text: Electronic attack activity is interfering with radar operations. What is the required first operational response?

choices.text: Request suspension of the activity through the appropriate channel for the en route or terminal environment.
Continue normal operations and wait for the military unit to stop on its own.
Shut down the radar without coordinating with any other facility.
Tell all aircraft to cancel IFR until the activity ends.

items.explanation: When EA affects operational use of radar, the activity must be suspended through the applicable coordination path.

items.question_text: Which aircraft must have merging target procedures applied even below 10,000 feet?

choices.text: Turbojet and presidential aircraft that are radar identified.
Piston aircraft in Class B airspace, regardless of target type.
Aircraft that have already been issued visual separation, regardless of target type.
VFR aircraft receiving basic radar service, regardless of target type.

items.explanation: Merging target procedures apply to all radar-identified aircraft at 10,000 feet and above, plus turbojets and presidential aircraft regardless of altitude.

items.question_text: What is your responsibility when aircraft are holding in an outer-fix holding pattern area shown on your scope?

choices.text: Provide radar surveillance of the holding airspace, watch for aircraft straying outside it, and help them return.
Monitor only the inbound course and ignore deviations once the aircraft enters the hold.
Suspend radar monitoring because the published hold provides all needed protection.
Transfer responsibility entirely to the pilot once the aircraft reports established in holding.

items.explanation: The paragraph requires surveillance of the depicted holding airspace and assistance if an aircraft strays outside it.

items.question_text: An aircraft is obviously going to deviate from its protected airspace area. What should you do?

choices.text: Inform the aircraft and, if necessary, assist it back to the assigned protected airspace.
Wait until the aircraft actually exits the protected airspace before saying anything.
Issue no advisory unless the pilot reports a navigation failure.
Terminate radar service immediately without further assistance.

items.explanation: Deviation advisories are issued when the aircraft's observed position and track will obviously cause a deviation from protected airspace.

items.question_text: When flight-progress recording components of the en route automation FDP are not operational, what manual entry is required?

choices.text: Record the observed or reported time over a fix at least once for each controlled aircraft in the sector.
Record every heading change in five-minute increments.
Stop providing radar services until the FDP returns.
Record only the time the aircraft enters and exits the ARTCC.

items.explanation: The paragraph requires manual fix posting at least once for each controlled aircraft when the FDP flight-progress recording components are down.

items.question_text: After ATC tells an aircraft "radar contact," when does normal compulsory position reporting resume?

choices.text: When ATC later informs the pilot that radar contact is lost or radar service is terminated.
As soon as the aircraft passes the next compulsory reporting point.
Only after the aircraft leaves controlled airspace.
When the pilot requests a frequency change.

items.explanation: Radar contact suspends normal compulsory reporting until ATC advises that radar contact is lost or radar service is terminated.

items.question_text: Which is one case where radar service is automatically terminated and the aircraft does not need to be advised separately?

choices.text: An instrument, visual, or contact approach aircraft has landed or has been instructed to change to advisory frequency, as applicable.
Any aircraft leaves your sector boundary, regardless of the service being provided.
A VFR aircraft enters Class B airspace.
A pilot acknowledges the last traffic advisory.

items.explanation: The paragraph lists specific situations where radar service ends automatically without a separate termination advisory.

items.question_text: What aircraft are eligible to receive beacon code assignments?

choices.text: Aircraft equipped with ADS-B and/or a transponder.
Any aircraft receiving IFR service, even if it has no transponder or ADS-B capability.
Military aircraft operating above FL180, even without beacon capability.
Aircraft already in radar contact, even without beacon capability.

items.explanation: Beacon code assignments are limited to aircraft equipped to use them.

items.question_text: When may you request an aircraft to change from the code it was squawking in the transferring facility's area?

choices.text: Not until the aircraft is within your area of responsibility unless an LOA, directive, or coordination says otherwise.
As soon as the automated handoff is initiated.
Any time after the pilot checks on frequency.
Only after the aircraft lands.

items.explanation: Beacon code changes normally wait until the aircraft is inside your area unless prior coordination or a standing procedure allows otherwise.

items.question_text: A pilot declares an emergency and the aircraft is not radar identified. Which code should be assigned?
items.explanation: Emergency aircraft that are not radar identified are assigned code 7700.

items.question_text: You observe code 7600. What set of procedures applies?

choices.text: Communications failure procedures.
Hijack procedures.
Lost link UAS procedures.
Standard VFR beacon reassignment procedures.

items.explanation: Code 7600 indicates radio failure, so communications-failure procedures apply.

items.question_text: You observe code 7500. What set of procedures applies?

choices.text: Hijack or unlawful interference procedures.
Communications failure procedures.
Emergency descent procedures.
Practice instrument approach procedures.

items.explanation: Code 7500 is handled as hijack or unlawful interference.

items.question_text: When a UAS displays code 7400, what is one of the controller's required actions?

choices.text: Determine the lost-link procedure and coordinate as needed so the UAS can execute it safely.
Treat the code the same as 7700 and disregard the programmed procedure.
Instruct the pilot to squawk 1200 immediately.
Assume the aircraft will continue on its present heading and altitude with no coordination.

items.explanation: Code 7400 is tied to lost-link UAS procedures, so the controller must identify and support the programmed lost-link procedure.

items.question_text: An IFR aircraft cancels IFR and is not requesting radar advisories. What should you tell the pilot to do with the beacon code?

choices.text: Squawk VFR, normally 1200.
Remain on the assigned IFR code until landing.
Squawk 7700 until leaving controlled airspace.
Turn the transponder off immediately.

items.explanation: Without continuing radar advisory service, the aircraft should be told to squawk VFR.

items.question_text: What is the normal rule for the special-use code assigned to pressure-suit flights and aircraft operating above FL600?

choices.text: Keep the aircraft on that special-use code unless one of the listed exceptions requires a change.
Change the aircraft to a standard discrete code at every center boundary.
Use 1200 whenever the aircraft descends below FL600.
Turn the transponder off after leaving Class A airspace.

items.explanation: These special flights normally remain on the reserved special-use code.

items.question_text: What should exercise FAKER aircraft do with their filed discrete beacon code?

choices.text: Remain on the exercise flight-plan discrete code.
Change to 1200 when entering the ARTCC.
Change to 7700 whenever NORAD requests an intercept profile.
Use any DOD code selected by the pilot.

items.explanation: FAKER aircraft stay on the exercise flight-plan discrete code.

items.question_text: When may you tell an aircraft on an assigned code to squawk standby?

choices.text: When it is about 15 miles from destination and you no longer want the transponder or ADS-B operating, or when needed to reduce clutter and it can be restored promptly.
Any time the aircraft enters Class A airspace.
Only after the aircraft has landed.
Whenever you want to verify altitude reporting performance.

items.explanation: Standby use is limited to the listed operational cases and should be returned to normal as soon as practical.

items.question_text: Unless your area of responsibility includes only Class A airspace, which VFR-related codes must also be monitored?

choices.text: 4000 and 7700 only.
Any code except a discrete computer-assigned code.

items.explanation: Code-monitor duties include the listed VFR-related codes unless the area contains only Class A airspace.

items.question_text: An assigned beacon code is not displaying, or the aircraft's transponder appears inoperative. What follow-up is required after advising the pilot?

choices.text: Ensure the next control position or next facility is notified if the transponder is malfunctioning or inoperative.
No further coordination is required once the pilot acknowledges.
Cancel radar service immediately and terminate the flight plan.
Tell the pilot to continue without a code and leave the data block unchanged.

items.explanation: The paragraph requires notification of the subsequent control position or next facility when the transponder is malfunctioning or inoperative.

items.question_text: If the ground interrogator appears inoperative or malfunctioning, what must you do?

choices.text: Inform the aircraft concerned that the beacon interrogator is inoperative or malfunctioning.
Tell the pilot to stop using the transponder permanently.
Switch all aircraft to primary-radar service only with no advisory.
Declare an emergency for all affected aircraft.

items.explanation: Aircraft concerned must be informed when the ground interrogator appears inoperative or malfunctioning.

items.question_text: On what basis may you disapprove a request, or withdraw a prior approval, to operate with a failed transponder or ADS-B Out?

choices.text: Traffic conditions or other operational factors.
Pilot preference only.
Aircraft age or maintenance history by itself.
Whether the destination has a control tower by itself.

items.explanation: The paragraph makes traffic conditions and operational factors the controlling basis for approval decisions.

items.question_text: Which event requires you to validate a Mode C altitude readout?

choices.text: An initial track start, a start from coast or frozen status, or an unreliable Mode C readout.
Every time a pilot checks on frequency, with no exceptions.
Only after the aircraft reports leaving cruise altitude.
Only when the aircraft enters Class A airspace.

items.explanation: The paragraph lists specific times when Mode C must be validated, including track starts and unreliable readouts.

items.question_text: When may you omit asking a Mode C aircraft to verify assigned altitude on initial contact?

choices.text: When the pilot states the assigned altitude, or a valid Mode C readout shows the aircraft established at the assigned altitude, among the listed exceptions.
Only when the aircraft is above FL180.
Only if the previous sector already issued a traffic advisory.
Never; verification is mandatory on every first call.

items.explanation: Altitude verification on initial contact is waived in the listed cases, including when the pilot already states the assigned altitude or valid Mode C confirms it.

items.question_text: In the USA, what additional step is required when confirming assigned altitude with a non-Mode C aircraft?

choices.text: Reconfirm all pilot altitude read backs.
Assign a new altitude immediately after the readback.
Require the pilot to report each compulsory point until landing.
Transfer the aircraft to the next sector before acknowledging the readback.

items.explanation: For non-Mode C aircraft in the USA, all pilot altitude read backs are reconfirmed.

items.question_text: If you want an aircraft to start or stop automatic altitude reporting, what must you do?

choices.text: Explicitly instruct the pilot to squawk altitude or stop altitude squawk.
Assume the pilot will turn altitude reporting on automatically after any beacon code change.
Tell the pilot to recycle the transponder without mentioning altitude reporting.
Wait for the pilot to offer the change without instruction.

items.explanation: The controller must directly tell the pilot when altitude reporting should be on or off.

items.question_text: Between 10,000 feet and 18,000 feet, when may you approve an inflight deviation request from an aircraft that has no transponder equipment installed?

choices.text: Only in an emergency.
Whenever the pilot agrees to remain VFR.
Whenever the aircraft stays below 12,500 feet.
Whenever the aircraft can maintain radio contact with ATC.

items.explanation: Except in an emergency, inflight deviation requests from aircraft without transponder equipment installed are not approved in that airspace.

items.question_text: If you want an aircraft's transponder and ADS-B Out turned off, what instruction applies?

choices.text: Stop squawk.
Squawk standby and ident.
Stop altitude squawk only.
Resume normal speed.

items.explanation: Beacon termination is accomplished by instructing the pilot to stop squawk.

items.question_text: For a terminal position, how high must the upper altitude filter limit be set at minimum?

choices.text: No lower than 1,000 feet above the highest altitude for which the controller is responsible.
Exactly at the highest assigned altitude in the sector.
At least 2,000 feet above the field elevation, regardless of sector limits.
No higher than the MVA for the sector.

items.explanation: Terminal altitude filters must encompass the controller's jurisdiction, including at least 1,000 feet above the highest altitude responsibility.

items.question_text: A malfunctioning ADS-B transmitter is jeopardizing the safe execution of ATC functions. What should you do?

choices.text: Instruct the aircraft to stop ADS-B transmissions and notify the OS/CIC.
Tell the pilot to ignore the problem unless another facility complains.
Assign 1200 and continue without any notification.
Terminate the flight plan immediately.

items.explanation: When the malfunction affects safe ATC operations, ADS-B transmissions can be stopped and the OS/CIC must be notified.

items.question_text: What does a call sign mismatch ADS-B alert mean?

choices.text: The transmitted ADS-B flight identification does not match the flight-plan aircraft identification.
The aircraft is using the wrong emergency code.
The ADS-B transmitter has stopped sending altitude information.
Two aircraft are operating on the same discrete beacon code.

items.explanation: A CSMM alert compares the transmitted ADS-B flight ID with the aircraft identification in the flight plan.

items.question_text: If a pilot asks you for ADS-B Out OFF operations, what may you do?

choices.text: Do not approve the request; notify the OS/CIC with the aircraft call sign and location.
Approve it if the pilot says the operation is mission-essential.
Approve it below 10,000 feet only.
Approve it if radar service is available from another source.

items.explanation: Controllers do not approve ADS-B Out OFF operations; the request goes to the OS/CIC.

items.question_text: Before providing radar service, what must be established and maintained?

choices.text: Radar identification of the aircraft involved, except for the listed exceptions.
A visual sighting of the aircraft from the tower or center window.
A pilot report over every compulsory reporting point.
An assigned heading matching the flight-plan route.

items.explanation: Radar service starts with established and maintained radar identification unless a specific exception applies.

items.question_text: Which is an approved primary-radar identification method?

choices.text: Observe a target whose position matches a direct position report relative to a known fix and whose track is consistent with the reported route or heading.
Assume the closest target to the airport is the correct one whenever departure traffic is light.
Use the aircraft's filed route by itself without correlating any target.
Identify the aircraft only after it reports the runway in sight.

items.explanation: Primary-radar identification can be established by correlating the observed target with a direct position report and consistent track.

items.question_text: Which is an approved beacon or ADS-B identification method?

choices.text: Request the pilot to activate IDENT and observe the identification display.
Assume any aircraft squawking a non-discrete code is the one you want.
Use a code change only after the aircraft lands.
Treat standby squawk as an emergency unless the pilot explains otherwise.

items.explanation: IDENT, code change, standby/normal response, and certain automated associations are approved identification methods.

items.question_text: When may a terminal auto-acquired aircraft be considered identified?

choices.text: When the data block is visible to you and either the target identity has been confirmed by an identification method or the automated handoff status meets the paragraph's conditions.
As soon as any data block appears, regardless of status symbols.
Only after the aircraft has crossed the first radar handoff boundary.
Only after tower issues a landing clearance.

items.explanation: Terminal automation identification depends on both the visible data block and a qualifying confirmation condition.

items.question_text: If target identification is questionable, what must you do?

choices.text: Take immediate action to re-identify the aircraft or terminate radar service.
Continue radar service until the pilot reports another fix.
Assume the full data block association remains correct unless proven otherwise.
Wait for the next controller to resolve the doubt.

items.explanation: Questionable identification requires immediate re-identification or termination of radar service.

items.question_text: When must an aircraft be given position information after radar identification is established?

choices.text: When identification is established by identifying turns or by a beacon identification method.
Only when identification is established by position correlation.
Only after the pilot requests vectors.
Never, because the aircraft can infer its own position from the code assignment.

items.explanation: Position information is required after identification by identifying turns or beacon identification methods, with the listed exceptions.

items.question_text: When should you tell a pilot "radar contact"?

choices.text: When initial radar identification is established, or when radar identification is reestablished after loss of contact or termination of service.
Only after the aircraft has accepted a handoff from another facility.
Only after the aircraft is on an IFR flight plan.
Whenever the pilot checks on frequency, even before identification.

items.explanation: Radar contact is announced when identification is first established or later reestablished.

items.question_text: At a minimum, what must an en route data block display while you retain it to maintain aircraft identity?

choices.text: Flight identification and altitude information.
Ground speed and destination airport only.
Wake category and final approach fix.
Pilot name and route amendment history.

items.explanation: The en route data block must display flight identification and altitude information at a minimum.

items.question_text: For terminal operations, when is retention of a VFR aircraft's data block no longer required in delegated airspace extending beyond Class B or Class C?

choices.text: When the aircraft is clear of Class B and Class C airspace and radar services have been terminated.
Only after the aircraft lands at a towered airport.
As soon as the aircraft leaves the final approach area.
When the aircraft squawks standby.

items.explanation: The note allows the terminal data block to be dropped after the aircraft is clear of the relevant airspace and radar service has ended.

items.question_text: If interfacility or intrafacility radar identification transfers are not operationally feasible, how must that limitation be handled?

choices.text: It must be covered in an LOA or coordinated by the controllers for a specified period of time.
It may be ignored if both controllers can see the same target.
It requires all aircraft to revert immediately to nonradar operations facility-wide.
It requires the receiving controller to accept all entries without handoff.

items.explanation: When routine transfer is not feasible, the workaround must be explicitly handled by LOA or direct coordination.

items.question_text: What is a point out?

choices.text: A transfer of radar identification to another controller without transferring radio communications.
A handoff in which the receiving controller also assumes the radio frequency immediately.
A traffic advisory issued directly to the pilot.
A method of terminating radar service to a VFR aircraft.

items.explanation: A point out transfers radar identification only; radio communications remain with the original controller.

items.question_text: Which is an approved method for transferring radar identification?

choices.text: Use landline voice communications, physical pointing, or approved automation capabilities.
Rely on pilot position reports alone without controller-to-controller transfer.
Use tower Quick Look functions whenever convenient, even without a facility directive.
Transfer identity only after the aircraft changes to the new frequency.

items.explanation: The paragraph lists the approved methods for transferring radar identification.

items.question_text: When you use the term "traffic" to coordinate separation, who must ensure approved separation is maintained?

choices.text: The controller accepting the restrictions.
Only the controller issuing the traffic call.
The pilot receiving the traffic advisory.
The supervisor, not the sector controllers.

items.explanation: The issuing controller must state the restrictions, and the accepting controller must ensure approved separation is maintained.

items.question_text: Once a handoff has been initiated or accepted, when may the transferring controller change the aircraft's flight path, altitude, speed, or data block without further approval?

choices.text: Only when otherwise coordinated or specified in an LOA or facility directive; otherwise the receiving controller's approval must be obtained first.
Any time before the pilot checks on with the receiving controller.
Any time the aircraft remains above the MVA.
Only after the aircraft enters the receiving sector physically.

items.explanation: The transferring controller must get the receiving controller's approval before making post-handoff changes unless a standing procedure allows it.

items.question_text: Before accepting a handoff, what must the receiving controller verify?

choices.text: That the target position matches the transferring controller's description or that the automated association is appropriate.
That the pilot has already acknowledged the next frequency.
That the aircraft has crossed the sector boundary.
That wake turbulence separation has been delegated to tower.

items.explanation: The receiving controller must ensure the target association is correct before accepting the handoff.

items.question_text: Before permitting an aircraft to enter another controller's delegated airspace on a point out, what must the transferring controller obtain?

choices.text: Approval from the receiving controller.
A beacon code change from the pilot.
A new IFR clearance from the overlying center.
A second radar identification method from the aircraft.

items.explanation: A point out requires the receiving controller's approval before the aircraft enters that delegated airspace.

items.question_text: When may automated information transfer replace verbal coordination?

choices.text: During radar handoff, via information displayed in full data blocks, and only when following the facility AIT directive or LOA.
Any time both sectors are using the same display software.
Only for VFR aircraft under basic radar service.
Whenever a fourth-line entry exists, regardless of directive coverage.

items.explanation: AIT works only within the specific handoff and directive conditions defined in the paragraph.

items.question_text: When may prearranged coordination be approved for aircraft entering another controller's area of jurisdiction?

choices.text: Only when the procedures are established and published in a facility directive.
Whenever the controllers have worked together before and understand the traffic flow.
Only when the aircraft is VFR.
Whenever the airspace boundary is not depicted on the display.

items.explanation: Prearranged coordination must be formalized in a facility directive; informal habit is not enough.

items.question_text: What must be done with free text entered on the en route fourth line before identification transfer is initiated?

choices.text: Remove it.
Convert it to a point out message automatically.
Leave it in place so the next sector has all possible context.
Copy it into the route field.

items.explanation: Free text may be used for team management, but it must be removed before identification transfer begins.

items.question_text: To which RNAV aircraft must radar separation be applied under this section?

choices.text: All RNAV aircraft operating at and below FL450 on Q routes or random RNAV routes, excluding oceanic airspace.
RNAV aircraft above FL450.
Aircraft on published victor airways regardless of RNAV route type.
Aircraft using point-to-point impromptu routes regardless of RNAV route type.

items.explanation: The application paragraph specifically calls out RNAV aircraft on Q routes and random RNAV routes at and below FL450.

items.question_text: How is target separation applied between digital targets on all-digital displays?

choices.text: Between the centers of the digital targets, without allowing them to touch.
Between the leading edges only.
By treating every digital target as if it were a beacon control slash.
Only by vertical separation, never laterally.

items.explanation: For all-digital displays, the digital targets are separated center-to-center and must not touch.

items.question_text: What additional controller actions are mandatory when target resolution is used?

choices.text: Issue mandatory traffic advisories and safety alerts.
Switch both aircraft to nonradar service.
Suspend all automated handoffs.
Require both aircraft to ident every 30 seconds.

items.explanation: Target resolution carries mandatory traffic-advisory and safety-alert responsibilities.

items.question_text: Using terminal single-sensor ASR or DTAS, what radar separation minimum applies when the aircraft are less than 40 miles from the antenna?

choices.text: 3 miles.
1 mile.
5 miles.
10 miles.

items.explanation: Single-sensor ASR or DTAS uses 3 miles inside 40 miles from the antenna.

items.question_text: When may you assign an altitude to an aircraft after another aircraft previously occupied that altitude?

choices.text: After the first aircraft has been issued a climb or descent and is observed with valid Mode C, or reports, leaving that altitude.
Immediately after you transmit the new altitude, if both aircraft are radar identified.
Only when both aircraft are above FL290.
Only after the first aircraft crosses the next sector boundary.

items.explanation: Vertical application allows reuse of an altitude only after the first aircraft has demonstrably left it under the paragraph's standards.

items.question_text: In which case may Mode C not be used to effect vertical separation?

choices.text: When the aircraft is on a cruise clearance or a contact approach.
When both aircraft are RVSM approved.
When both aircraft are radar identified and below FL180.
When the pilot reports leaving the altitude promptly.

items.explanation: The exceptions paragraph expressly bars using Mode C for vertical separation in several cases, including cruise clearances and contact approaches.

items.question_text: When may passing or diverging separation replace other approved separation in terminal operations?

choices.text: When the aircraft have passed or crossed as required, their courses or vectors diverge by at least 15 degrees, and the tracks are monitored so the applicable targets will not touch.
Whenever the aircraft are more than 1 mile apart and both pilots have each other in sight.
Only when the aircraft are on the same final approach course.
Only when the aircraft are climbing and descending through each other's altitude.

items.explanation: Passing or diverging separation has specific observation, divergence, and monitoring requirements.

items.question_text: How much supplemental radar separation is added for a standard formation flight from other aircraft?

choices.text: Add 1 mile to the applicable radar separation minima.
Add 2 miles to all minima.
Add 5 miles only if the formation is military.
No extra separation is required for standard formations.

items.explanation: Standard formations require one additional mile over the applicable radar minima.

items.question_text: In terminal operations, what obstruction separation minimum applies when aircraft are less than 40 miles from the antenna?

choices.text: 3 miles from the prominent obstruction.
1.5 miles from the prominent obstruction.
5 miles from the prominent obstruction.
10 miles from the prominent obstruction.

items.explanation: Terminal obstruction separation uses 3 miles inside 40 miles from the antenna.

items.question_text: If VFR aircraft are being provided Class B, Class C, or TRSA services near adjacent airspace, what is the boundary requirement?

choices.text: Ensure the targets do not touch the boundary of the adjacent airspace.
Apply the full nonradar boundary minima used for IFR aircraft.
No boundary protection is needed because they are VFR.
Keep them at least 5 miles from every adjacent boundary.

items.explanation: The specific boundary minima in subparagraphs a and b do not apply to those VFR services; instead, the targets must not touch the boundary.

items.question_text: When an aircraft has been tracked to the edge of scope and nonradar separation is not yet established, what minimum applies less than 40 miles from the antenna?

choices.text: 3 miles from the edge of scope.
1 mile from the edge of scope.
5 miles from the edge of scope.
10 miles from the edge of scope.

items.explanation: Edge-of-scope separation is 3 miles inside 40 miles from the antenna.

items.question_text: What correction factor is added when using a display with a previously specified beacon target displacement?

choices.text: Add 1 mile to the applicable separation minima.
Add 0.5 mile to the applicable separation minima.
Subtract 1 mile because the displacement is already accounted for.
No correction factor is allowed.

items.explanation: Specified beacon target displacement requires a 1-mile correction factor.

items.question_text: When may aircraft be vectored in Class G airspace?

choices.text: Only on pilot request, and only as an additional service.
Whenever ATC wants a shortcut through uncontrolled airspace.
Whenever the aircraft is receiving IFR service.
Only when the weather is below VFR minima.

items.explanation: Vectoring in Class G airspace is limited to pilot-requested additional service.

items.question_text: When you initiate a vector, what should you normally tell the pilot in addition to the heading or turn?

choices.text: The purpose of the vector and, if appropriate, what to expect when radar guidance ends.
The name of every adjacent sector the aircraft may enter next.
The exact ground speed reduction expected from the vector.
That the vector cancels all future published restrictions.

items.explanation: Vectoring instructions include the method of vectoring plus the purpose and any relevant expectations for when guidance terminates.

items.question_text: After departure or a missed approach, when may a radar facility vector an aircraft below the MVA or MIA under the special provision?

choices.text: No prominent obstacles may be within 10 NM of the departure end of runway, and the aircraft must be allowed an uninterrupted climb to meet the MVA or MIA within 10 NM.
The pilot must agree to maintain visual separation from terrain.
Any vector below the MVA or MIA is allowed if the aircraft is on a SID.
The aircraft must already be above the MEA for the first route segment.

items.explanation: The below-minimum vectoring provision has specific obstacle and climb conditions; it is not a general shortcut.

items.question_text: What is the basic rule when applying speed adjustments?

choices.text: Keep them to the minimum necessary, avoid alternating decreases and increases, and terminate them when no longer needed.
Prefer large speed changes so spacing is corrected immediately.
Alternate increases and decreases to fine-tune spacing continuously.
Maintain ATC-assigned speed until landing unless the pilot cancels it.

items.explanation: The application paragraph sets the overall discipline for speed control: minimum necessary, no unnecessary oscillation, and terminate when done.

items.question_text: Which is an approved speed-control instruction?

choices.text: Maintain maximum forward speed.
Use whatever speed best fits the spacing and advise me later.
Ignore published speed restrictions until final approach.
Fly the fastest speed the aircraft ever certified for.

items.explanation: The methods paragraph lists the approved forms of speed instruction, including maximum forward speed.

items.question_text: What is the maximum speed that should be assigned to aircraft operating beneath Class B airspace or in a VFR corridor through Class B?

choices.text: 200 knots.
210 knots.
230 knots.
250 knots.

items.explanation: The speed-assignment table limits those aircraft to not more than 200 knots.

items.question_text: When may you cancel an ATC-assigned speed adjustment and return the aircraft to normal speed?

choices.text: When ATC-assigned speed adjustments are no longer needed and no published speed restriction applies.
Whenever you want the pilot to ignore all future route or procedure speed limits.
Only after the aircraft lands.
Whenever the pilot reaches 10,000 feet MSL.

items.explanation: Returning an aircraft to normal speed is appropriate only when ATC-assigned speed control is no longer needed and no published speed restriction still applies.

items.question_text: When vectoring a departing aircraft on a radar SID, what is not permitted?

choices.text: Concurrent use of a diverse vector area (DVA).
Assigning headings within the published heading range on the radar SID.
Vectoring after takeoff when the SID says heading assigned by ATC.
Using the radar SID at all.

items.explanation: A radar SID and a DVA cannot be used concurrently.

items.question_text: If a departing aircraft will be vectored immediately after takeoff, what must the initial heading match?

choices.text: Either the SID being flown or the DVA, if one applies.
Any heading that points generally toward the filed route.
The final approach course for arrivals to the same airport.
A heading chosen by the pilot for terrain avoidance.

items.explanation: Initial headings for immediate post-takeoff vectoring must match the SID or DVA framework, and SID/DVA cannot be used concurrently.

items.question_text: Before using successive or simultaneous departure radar minima, what baseline condition must be met?

choices.text: Radar identification must be established within 1 mile of the takeoff runway end, helipad, or vertipad, and the courses must diverge by at least the required amount.
Both aircraft must be airborne and 3 miles apart before takeoff roll begins.
The departures must use different beacon codes and different departure frequencies.
Both aircraft must remain on runway heading for at least 5 miles.

items.explanation: Those departure minima depend on rapid radar identification and sufficient course divergence.

items.question_text: When releasing a departure in front of an arriving aircraft on final, what radar separation is required under the basic rule?

choices.text: At least 2 miles, provided it will increase to 3 miles, or 5 miles when 40 miles or more from the antenna, within 1 minute after takeoff.
Exactly 1 mile as soon as the departure starts takeoff roll.
At least 5 miles in every case regardless of antenna distance.
Only vertical separation is required; no mileage minimum applies.

items.explanation: The departure-arrival rule permits a 2-mile release only when the separation will promptly build to the required radar minimum.

items.question_text: For simultaneous operations between a departure and an arrival on another parallel or nonintersecting diverging runway, how must the departure course diverge from the missed approach course?

choices.text: Immediately by at least 30 degrees until separation is applied.
By at least 15 degrees after the aircraft reaches pattern altitude.
Only after the departure is 2 miles from the runway.
No divergence is required if the runways are more than 2,500 feet apart.

items.explanation: The basic authorization requires immediate 30-degree divergence from the missed approach course.

items.question_text: When may an aircraft be vectored to intercept the final approach course closer than 2 miles outside the approach gate?

choices.text: When the reported ceiling is at least 500 feet above the MVA or MIA and visibility is at least 3 miles, but not closer than the approach gate.
Whenever the airport has an operating control tower.
Only after the aircraft has crossed the final approach fix.
Any time the pilot is flying an RNAV approach.

items.explanation: The normal vector point is at least 2 miles outside the gate, with limited exceptions.

items.question_text: When the interception point is 2 miles or more from the approach gate, what is the maximum final approach course interception angle for most aircraft?

choices.text: 30 degrees.
20 degrees.
45 degrees.
60 degrees.

items.explanation: The table limits the normal interception angle to 30 degrees when the intercept point is 2 miles or more from the gate.

items.question_text: What must you tell an aircraft if a vector will take it across the final approach course?

choices.text: That vectors across final are expected, and the reason for the action.
Only the heading change; the reason should never be stated.
That it should turn inbound on its own whenever it sees the course needle center.
That the approach clearance is canceled automatically.

items.explanation: Vectors across final require an explicit advisory and the reason for the crossing action.

items.question_text: What must be issued before an aircraft reaches the approach gate, except when conducting a radar approach?

choices.text: Position relative to the final-approach reference, any needed vector to intercept, and the approach clearance.
Only a speed reduction and the destination ATIS code.
Only the missed approach procedure and tower frequency.
Nothing, as long as the aircraft is already descending on the MVA.

items.explanation: Arrival instructions before the gate include position, vector if needed, and approach clearance.

items.question_text: Who is normally responsible for separation of radar arrivals during the approach-control function?

choices.text: The radar controller performing the approach-control function, unless the tower is providing visual separation or an LOA or facility directive provides otherwise.
The tower local controller in every case.
The pilot once established on final.
The receiving center after handoff to terminal.

items.explanation: Approach-separation responsibility belongs to the radar controller performing the approach-control function unless a listed exception applies.

items.question_text: When runway centerlines are at least 2,500 feet but no more than 3,600 feet apart, what diagonal minimum applies between successive aircraft on adjacent final approach courses during simultaneous dependent approaches?

choices.text: 1 mile.
1.5 miles.
2 miles.
3 miles.

items.explanation: The dependent-approach table uses 1 mile diagonal spacing in that runway-spacing band.

items.question_text: For dual simultaneous independent approaches, what runway-centerline spacing is normally required if there is no offset-approach relief provision in use?

choices.text: At least 3,600 feet.
At least 2,500 feet.
At least 7,500 feet.
At least 9,000 feet.

items.explanation: Dual simultaneous independent approaches normally require 3,600-foot runway-centerline spacing unless the offset-approach provision applies.

items.question_text: When may PRM approaches be conducted?

choices.text: Only when the procedure is charted in the approach title and the instrument approach charts specifically authorize simultaneous approaches.
Whenever parallel runways are available and the weather is VMC.
Only at military airfields.
Only when the tower requests additional arrival capacity.

items.explanation: PRM approaches are not ad hoc; they must be specifically charted and authorized.

items.question_text: During simultaneous offset instrument approaches, when must the transfer to tower frequency be completed?

choices.text: Before losing vertical separation between the aircraft.
After both aircraft are established on final approach courses.
Only after the lead straight-in aircraft crosses the threshold.
At any point before touchdown, as long as the local controller is briefed.

items.explanation: The communications transfer timing is specifically tied to vertical separation in SOIA operations.

items.question_text: For simultaneous independent approaches to widely spaced parallel runways without final monitors, what runway-centerline spacing threshold applies when field elevation is at or below 5,000 feet MSL?

choices.text: More than 9,000 feet.
At least 7,500 feet.
At least 4,300 feet.
At least 3,600 feet.

items.explanation: The widely spaced parallel rule uses more than 9,000 feet at lower field elevations, or 9,200 feet above 5,000 feet MSL.

items.question_text: If one aircraft goes around while reduced-separation simultaneous approaches are in progress, what must happen before the approved reduced separation is lost?

choices.text: Control instructions must be issued expeditiously to increase separation and establish approved separation.
The controller may wait to see whether the aircraft rejoins the final approach course.
The tower assumes all separation responsibility immediately with no further radar instruction.
Only wake turbulence advisories are required.

items.explanation: The transitional procedure requires immediate control action to build approved separation before the reduced standard is lost.

items.question_text: What does a pilot's acceptance of a radar approach not waive?

choices.text: The prescribed weather minima for the airport or the aircraft operator.
The requirement to maintain two-way communications.
The need for a transponder code.
The missed approach procedure.

items.explanation: The pilot remains responsible for determining whether the approach and landing are authorized under the applicable weather minima.

items.question_text: Which item is part of the information package for an aircraft that will conduct a radar approach?

choices.text: The altimeter setting.
A clearance to descend below published minima at pilot discretion.
A cancellation of all lost-communications procedures.
A guarantee that weather updates will stop once final descent begins.

items.explanation: Radar approach information includes the altimeter setting and the other specified approach-information items.

items.question_text: After a no-gyro approach has turned onto final and before reaching the approach gate, what turn rate should be assigned?

choices.text: Half-standard rate turns.
Standard rate turns.
No turns at all.
Maximum practical bank angle.

items.explanation: Once on final and before the approach gate, the aircraft is told to make half-standard rate turns.

items.question_text: If an aircraft is likely to encounter IFR weather during a radar approach, when should lost-communications instructions be issued?

choices.text: As soon as possible after establishing radar identification and radio communications, subject to the listed repeated-approach exception.
Only after the aircraft reaches the final approach fix.
Only if the pilot reports the airport not in sight at decision height.
After the tower has issued landing clearance.

items.explanation: Lost-communications instructions are issued early in the radar approach setup when IFR weather is likely.

items.question_text: If radar contact is lost during an approach before the aircraft has started final approach, what should you do?

choices.text: Clear the aircraft to an appropriate NAVAID or fix for an instrument approach.
Continue the radar approach using the last observed heading.
Tell the aircraft to remain on present heading until visual contact is regained.
Cancel IFR and transfer the aircraft to advisory frequency.

items.explanation: Before final approach starts, loss of radar contact requires clearing the aircraft to an appropriate NAVAID or fix for an instrument approach.

items.question_text: When should the pilot be told to perform the landing check in a normal pattern for a radar approach?

choices.text: On downwind and in time to complete it before turning base.
After turning final.
Only after the tower issues landing clearance.
At touchdown rollout.

items.explanation: Landing check timing is tied to downwind and completion before base in a normal pattern.

items.question_text: How often must position information be given before starting final approach?

choices.text: At least once.
Every 30 seconds from downwind onward.
Only if the pilot reports unfamiliarity.
Only after the final controller changeover.

items.explanation: The aircraft must receive position information at least once before final approach begins.

items.question_text: What must be included when telling an aircraft to change frequency for final approach guidance?

choices.text: The name of the facility.
The assigned missed approach procedure.
The runway magnetic heading.
The next center frequency after landing.

items.explanation: Final-controller changeover instructions include the facility name.

items.question_text: What should the final controller ask for on initial contact?

choices.text: A communications check.
A beacon code change.
A report of runway in sight.
A request for descent clearance.

items.explanation: On initial contact with the final controller, the aircraft is asked for a communications check.

items.question_text: After contact with the final controller is established and the aircraft is on final approach course, what should be told to the aircraft regarding further transmissions?

choices.text: Do not acknowledge further transmissions.
Acknowledge every mile report with ident.
Change back to the previous approach frequency if workload permits.
Ignore all future tower clearances.

items.explanation: The procedure reduces unnecessary pilot workload on final by eliminating acknowledgments.

items.question_text: Before final descent for a full-stop landing when any portion of the final approach will be in IFR conditions, what must be issued?

choices.text: A specific missed approach procedure approved for the radar approach being conducted.
A visual approach clearance in case the pilot breaks out early.
A change to advisory frequency.
A speed assignment to minimum practical speed.

items.explanation: The missed approach procedure must be issued before final descent in those IFR conditions.

items.question_text: Before a low approach or touch-and-go begins final descent, what must be issued?

choices.text: Departure instructions to be followed upon completion of the approach.
A missed approach procedure identical to a full-stop landing.
A clearance to change to advisory frequency.
A transponder standby instruction.

items.explanation: The departure or climb-out instructions must be issued before final descent begins.

items.question_text: What must be obtained and then issued to an aircraft on final approach to an airport served by a tower?

choices.text: A clearance to land, touch and go, or make a low approach, along with the surface wind.
A visual approach clearance from the final controller only.
A handoff back to center before the runway threshold.
A mandatory switch to advisory frequency before touchdown.

items.explanation: The final controller obtains the tower clearance and passes it, along with the surface wind, to the aircraft.

items.question_text: If completion of a safe radar approach becomes questionable and the runway environment is not in sight, what action is required?

choices.text: Instruct the aircraft to execute the missed approach previously given, or to climb or maintain a specified altitude and fly a specified course.
Continue the approach unless the pilot specifically requests a missed approach.
Transfer the aircraft to tower and let tower decide.
Cancel IFR immediately and issue VFR instructions.

items.explanation: Final approach abnormalities require positive missed-approach or alternate climb-and-course instructions if the runway environment is not in sight.

items.question_text: After a military single-frequency approach has begun, when is a frequency change allowed?

choices.text: Only under one of the listed exceptions, such as after landing or low approach, in daylight VFR conditions, at pilot request, in an emergency, after visual approach clearance, or after IFR cancellation.
Whenever the receiving controller prefers a cleaner frequency split.
At any point after handoff, even if the pilot is still in IMC.
Only once the aircraft reaches decision height.

items.explanation: Single-frequency approaches are designed to avoid frequency changes after the approach begins except for the explicit exceptions.

items.question_text: When are recommended altitudes on a surveillance final approach provided?

choices.text: When the pilot requests them.
Automatically on every surveillance approach.
Only after the aircraft reports the runway in sight.
Only when the controller loses course accuracy.

items.explanation: Recommended altitudes are a requested service on surveillance approaches.

items.question_text: What report may be requested from an aircraft on a surveillance approach?

choices.text: Report runway, approach lights, runway lights, airport, or visual ability to proceed on a point-in-space surface route, as applicable.
Report beacon code changes every mile.
Report fuel remaining at decision height.
Report the precise crosswind component over the threshold.

items.explanation: Visual-reference reporting is specifically authorized for surveillance approaches and point-in-space operations.

items.question_text: If the surveillance approach will terminate in a circle-to-land maneuver, what must you obtain before issuing final descent?

choices.text: The aircraft approach category, so the applicable circling MDA can be issued.
The pilot's preferred runway exit.
The tower's landing clearance.
A request to proceed visually to the airport.

items.explanation: Circle-to-land surveillance approaches require the aircraft category so the correct circling MDA can be provided.

items.question_text: When the aircraft reaches the descent point on a surveillance approach with no descent restriction, what instruction should be issued?

choices.text: Descend to minimum descent altitude.
Descend below minimum descent altitude and report runway in sight.
Maintain present altitude until the missed approach point.
Cancel IFR and continue visually.

items.explanation: Absent a restriction altitude, the descent instruction is to descend to MDA at the descent point.

items.question_text: Approximately how often should transmissions occur with aircraft on surveillance final approach?

choices.text: About every 15 seconds.
About every 5 seconds.
About every 30 seconds.
Only each time the aircraft passes a mile point.

items.explanation: Surveillance final guidance is updated approximately every 15 seconds.

items.question_text: Which is one required reason to discontinue surveillance approach guidance?

choices.text: The continuation of a safe approach to the MAP is questionable.
The pilot asks for another weather update.
The tower has already issued landing clearance.
The aircraft is one mile from the runway.

items.explanation: Surveillance guidance must stop when requested by the pilot, when safe continuation is questionable, or when the aircraft is over the MAP.

items.question_text: When should an aircraft on a PAR approach be told it is approaching glidepath?

choices.text: Approximately 10 to 30 seconds before final descent.
At decision height.
Only after crossing the threshold.
Exactly one mile from touchdown.

items.explanation: Glidepath notification precedes final descent by roughly 10 to 30 seconds.

items.question_text: When must decision height be provided on a PAR approach?

choices.text: When the pilot requests it.
On every PAR approach whether requested or not.
Only after glideslope failure.
Only when the runway lights are out of service.

items.explanation: The controller provides DH when the pilot requests it.

items.question_text: What is the correct controller action when the aircraft reaches the point where final descent is to start on a PAR approach?

choices.text: Instruct the aircraft to begin descent.
Wait until it is over the approach lights before mentioning descent.
Issue a missed approach instruction first and then rescind it.
Transfer the aircraft to tower before beginning descent.

items.explanation: The descent instruction is issued when the aircraft reaches the point where final descent should begin.

items.question_text: Approximately how often should transmissions occur with aircraft on a precision final approach?

choices.text: About every 5 seconds.
About every 15 seconds.
About every 30 seconds.
Only at each mile from touchdown.

items.explanation: PAR final guidance is updated approximately every 5 seconds.

items.question_text: How often must distance from touchdown be given on a PAR final approach?

choices.text: At least once each mile.
Only every two miles.
Only at glideslope intercept and decision height.
Only if the pilot asks for it.

items.explanation: The aircraft is advised of distance from touchdown at least once every mile on final.

items.question_text: What must you tell the aircraft when it reaches the published decision height?

choices.text: At decision height.
Runway in sight.
Begin missed approach.
Contact tower after landing.

items.explanation: Decision-height notification is a required PAR call.

items.question_text: Until what point must glidepath and course information continue to be provided on a PAR approach?

choices.text: Until the aircraft passes over the threshold.
Only until decision height.
Only until the approach lights are in sight.
Only until one mile from touchdown.

items.explanation: Position advisories include continued glidepath and course information until the aircraft is over the threshold.

items.question_text: Which is one required reason to discontinue precision approach guidance?

choices.text: Continuation of a safe approach to the landing threshold is questionable.
The aircraft has reached one mile from touchdown.
The pilot reports the runway in sight but does not request or advise visual continuation.
The aircraft is below the glideslope but correcting slowly.

items.explanation: PAR guidance stops for the listed reasons, including when a safe continuation is questionable.

items.question_text: When should after-landing communication-transfer instructions normally be delayed on a PAR approach?

choices.text: Until the aircraft is on the landing rollout, to avoid distracting the pilot during transition and touchdown.
Until the aircraft has cleared the airport movement area.
Until the pilot reports parked at the gate.
They should never be delayed once landing clearance exists.

items.explanation: The note emphasizes delaying after-landing transfer instructions slightly until rollout.

items.question_text: If the elevation portion of PAR equipment fails during a precision approach, what is the first required action?

choices.text: Discontinue PAR instructions and tell the aircraft to take over visually or, if unable, execute a missed approach.
Continue the PAR as normal using azimuth only with no additional pilot advisory.
Transfer the aircraft to tower and let the pilot decide.
Climb the aircraft immediately to the MVA without further explanation.

items.explanation: Loss of elevation information immediately ends normal PAR guidance unless a surveillance approach can be established under the paragraph's follow-on procedures.

items.question_text: When may a PAR approach still be conducted if the ASR is unusable?

choices.text: When a nonradar instrument approach will position the aircraft over a NAVAID or DME fix within precision radar coverage, or an adjacent radar facility can provide a direct radar handoff to the PAR controller.
Only when the pilot reports the airport in sight before final approach.
Only when the aircraft is military and above the MVA.
Never; ASR failure cancels all PAR operations.

items.explanation: PAR can continue without ASR only through the specific positioning or direct-handoff conditions listed in the paragraph.

items.question_text: If another controller is involved in a CA or MCI alert, what should you do when immediate action is not dictated?

choices.text: Initiate coordination to ensure an effective course of action.
Suppress the alert first and coordinate only if the pilot asks for clarification.
Wait for the other controller to call you.
Assume the alert belongs entirely to the other controller.

items.explanation: CA and MCI alerts require prompt evaluation and coordination when another controller is involved, unless immediate action makes coordination impractical.

items.question_text: What is the first controller responsibility when an E-MSAW alert is displayed?

choices.text: Immediately analyze the situation and take appropriate action to resolve the alert.
Suppress the alert automatically and review it later.
Assume the alert is spurious until a pilot confirms terrain proximity.
Transfer the aircraft to another sector before acting.

items.explanation: E-MSAW alerts demand immediate analysis and action.

items.question_text: Before you establish communication with an aircraft outside your area of jurisdiction, when may assigned or interim altitude information be modified?

choices.text: Only if verbal coordination identifies who will modify the data block, or an LOA or facility directive provides otherwise.
Any time the aircraft is tagged in automation.
Only after the pilot has been assigned a new beacon code.
Whenever the conflict probe shows no conflict.

items.explanation: Altitude-field changes outside your jurisdiction require coordination unless a standing procedure authorizes them.

items.question_text: If Mode C altitude information is unavailable or unreliable during climb to or descent from FL180 and above, how often must reported altitudes be entered at minimum?

choices.text: At least each 10,000 feet.
At least each 5,000 feet.
Only at the top of climb or bottom of descent.
Only once after radar contact is established.

items.explanation: Reported altitude entry is required at least every 10,000 feet in that climb or descent band.

items.question_text: Where 1,000-foot vertical separation is applicable, how far above and below the sector's altitude stratum must selected altitude limits and display filters include Mode C targets and data blocks at a minimum?

choices.text: 1,200 feet above the highest and below the lowest altitude or flight level of the sector.
1,000 feet above and below only.
2,200 feet above and below in every case.
Only the exact altitude stratum of the sector.

items.explanation: The selected altitude limits expand beyond the sector stratum to support merging-target procedures.

items.question_text: When may the OK function be used to override sector eligibility?

choices.text: Only when prior coordination has been effected or the flight is within the control jurisdiction of the sector.
Whenever a full data block is displayed.
Only for VFR flights.
Whenever the pilot requests a shortcut.

items.explanation: Sector-eligibility override is tightly limited to the two conditions named in the paragraph.

items.question_text: How may coast tracks be used in applying radar or nonradar separation criteria?

choices.text: They may not be used.
They may be used only for nonradar separation.
They may be used only after verbal coordination.
They may be used if the aircraft is above FL180.

items.explanation: Coast tracks are not separation tools under either radar or nonradar criteria.

items.question_text: In what mode should controller-initiated coast tracks normally be started?

choices.text: In FLAT mode.
In free-track mode for all controlled aircraft.
In track-suspend mode.
In any mode, as long as the target is within radar coverage.

items.explanation: Controller-initiated coast tracks are started in FLAT mode, with only a narrow reminder-use exception for free coast tracking.

items.question_text: If ATC initiates a delay and issues holding instructions with an EFC other than "no delay expected," what must be entered into ERAM?

choices.text: A hold message, with EFC and any non-published holding information entered by the approved methods.
Only a free-text remark in the fourth line.
Only the aircraft type and route of flight.
Nothing, because the aircraft's hold can be inferred from the track history.

items.explanation: ATC-initiated holding delay with an EFC requires the hold information to be entered by the specified ERAM methods.

items.question_text: If "SAA DOWN" is displayed in the Outage View, what must sector controllers do?

choices.text: Manually create visual indicators on the situation display to reflect changes to special activity airspace status.
Assume the displayed SAA status will continue updating automatically.
Ignore the special activity airspace display until the outage clears.
Terminate all IFR operations that could be affected by SAA status.

items.explanation: SAA DOWN means updates stop, so controllers must manually reflect status changes on the display.

items.question_text: What may STARS be used for under this application paragraph?

choices.text: Identifying aircraft assigned a discrete beacon code, maintaining target identity, and performing handoffs of those targets between controllers.
Authorizing pilots to deviate from IFR altitude requirements.
Replacing all local directives for terminal operations.
Automatically issuing takeoff and landing clearances.

items.explanation: The application paragraph states the basic STARS uses for discrete-code target identification, identity maintenance, and handoffs.

items.question_text: What responsibility does terminal automation not remove from the controller?

choices.text: Ensuring proper identification, maintenance of identity, handoff of the correct target, and separation of aircraft.
Entering a data block for the aircraft after takeoff.
Monitoring weather and runway conditions.
Using the ATIS code in radio communications.

items.explanation: Automation does not transfer the controller's core responsibility for identity and separation.

items.question_text: Which is one authorized use of terminal automation?

choices.text: Handoff.
Independent issuance of IFR route clearances to pilots by the computer.
Automatic approval of deviations into another controller's airspace.
Elimination of the need for target identification.

items.explanation: Tracking, tagging, handoff, altitude information, coordination, ground speed, and identification are all listed functional uses.

items.question_text: When may the automatic altitude readout of an aircraft under another controller's jurisdiction be used for vertical separation purposes?

choices.text: Only when operating with single-site radar coverage or in FUSION mode, with prearranged coordination procedures in a facility directive, and not within a mosaic configuration.
Any time STARS is available and both controllers are on the same frequency.
Only after the pilot verbally confirms the altitude to both controllers.
Only for VFR aircraft in Class B airspace.

items.explanation: Cross-controller use of automatic altitude readout for vertical separation is tightly conditioned by display mode and prearranged coordination.

items.question_text: What designator format should normally be used for information displayed in STARS?

choices.text: Two-letter ICAO designators or three-letter designators as appropriate, unless program limitations require a single-letter alpha prefix.
Only FAA three-letter airport identifiers.
Only single-letter prefixes for every display.
Only full airport or facility names spelled out in plain language.

items.explanation: The display-format paragraph specifies the normal designator forms for STARS information.

items.question_text: When may the CA inhibit function be used in STARS terminal operations?

choices.text: Only for aircraft routinely engaged in operations where approved separation criteria do not apply.
Whenever two aircraft are within 5 miles of each other.
Whenever the local controller is too busy to respond to alerts.
Only after the alert has already caused a pilot deviation.

items.explanation: The inhibit function is limited to operations where approved separation criteria do not apply.

items.question_text: Which aircraft should normally have MSAW processing inhibited?

choices.text: VFR aircraft and aircraft that cancel IFR, unless the pilot specifically requests otherwise.
All IFR arrivals inside the final approach fix.
Only military aircraft above FL180.
Any aircraft receiving traffic advisories.

items.explanation: The terminal MSAW rule normally inhibits processing for VFR and IFR-cancel aircraft unless the pilot wants it retained.

items.question_text: When may the track suspend function be used?

choices.text: Only when data block overlap in holding patterns or near final approach creates an unworkable situation.
Whenever the controller wants a cleaner display during routine sequencing.
Only for VFR aircraft remaining outside Class B airspace.
Whenever the aircraft is more than 5 miles from the airport.

items.explanation: Track suspend is a narrow tool for display-overlap problems, not a routine display preference.

items.question_text: What does acknowledgment of an ARV alert signify?

choices.text: That appropriate action has been taken or will be taken.
That the alert has been determined false and may be ignored.
That runway assignment can no longer be changed.
That the alert has been transferred to tower responsibility only.

items.explanation: ARV acknowledgment signifies operational action, not dismissal of the alert.
